title: Java Cheatsheet
description: Master Java with clear, memorable concepts — explained like a friend teaching you

concepts.name: Your First Java Program
concepts.explanation: Think of this as Java's 'handshake ceremony.' Every Java program needs a class (a container for your code) and a main method (the starting gate where Java begins running). It's like a house: the class is the building, and main() is the front door.
keyPoints: public = anyone can access this (like a public park)
static = belongs to the class itself, no object needed
void = this method doesn't return anything
main = Java always looks for this method first
String[] args = command line arguments (optional inputs when running)

concepts.name: Primitive Data Types
concepts.explanation: Primitives are Java's building blocks — simple values stored directly in memory. Think of them as different-sized boxes: a byte is a tiny box for small numbers, while a long is a huge box for massive numbers. Use the smallest box that fits your data to save memory.
concepts.code:
// Whole numbers (pick based on size needed)
byte tiny = 127;           // -128 to 127 (1 byte)
short small = 32000;       // -32,768 to 32,767 (2 bytes)
int normal = 2000000000;   // Most common choice (4 bytes)
long huge = 9000000000L;   // Add 'L' suffix! (8 bytes)

// Decimal numbers
float price = 19.99f;      // Add 'f' suffix! (4 bytes)
double precise = 3.14159;  // Default for decimals (8 bytes)

// Others
boolean isReady = true;    // Only true or false
char letter = 'A';         // Single character in single quotes
keyPoints: int is your go-to for whole numbers — 99% of the time, it's enough
double is default for decimals — use float only when memory is tight
Don't forget L for long and f for float — compiler needs the hint!
boolean is named after George Boole — it's just yes/no, on/off, true/false

concepts.name: Variables & Constants
concepts.explanation: A variable is like a labeled box where you store values. A constant (using final) is a sealed box — once you put something in, it's locked forever. Use constants for values that should never change, like Pi or your app's version number.
keyPoints: final = 'this can never be changed' — use for true constants
Variable names start lowercase, constants are ALL_CAPS
Descriptive names > short names: userAge beats a, even if it's longer
Java is strongly typed — once a box is labeled 'int', it only holds ints

concepts.name: Reading User Input
concepts.explanation: Scanner is Java's way of listening to the user. It's like a microphone that captures what they type. Always close it when done — leaving it open is like leaving a water tap running.
keyPoints: nextLine() reads everything until Enter — best for text with spaces
nextInt(), nextDouble() read just the number — watch out for leftover newlines!
Common bug: After nextInt(), call nextLine() to consume the leftover Enter key
Scanner can also read from files — just pass File instead of System.in

concepts.name: Type Casting
concepts.explanation: Casting is like pouring liquid between containers. Pouring from a small cup to a big jug is safe (widening). But pouring from a big jug to a small cup might spill (narrowing) — you need to explicitly say 'I know this might lose data.'
keyPoints: Widening is automatic: byte → short → int → long → float → double
Narrowing needs (type) cast and can lose data — decimals are truncated, not rounded!
Overflow wraps around silently — 128 in a byte becomes -128
When in doubt, use the bigger type to avoid surprises

concepts.name: String Basics
concepts.explanation: Strings are sequences of characters wrapped in double quotes. Unlike primitives, they're objects with superpowers (methods). But here's the catch: Strings are immutable — every 'change' actually creates a brand new String.
keyPoints: Always use equals() to compare Strings, never == (compares memory addresses)
String methods return NEW Strings — the original never changes
Use + for simple concatenation, StringBuilder for loops (much faster)
null is not the same as "" — null means no string at all

concepts.name: Arithmetic Operators
concepts.explanation: These are your basic math tools. The only tricky one is division: when both numbers are integers, Java throws away the decimal part. It's like asking 'how many whole pizzas can 10 slices make if each pizza needs 3?' — answer is 3, not 3.33.
concepts.code:
int a = 10, b = 3;

int sum = a + b;        // 13
int diff = a - b;       // 7
int product = a * b;    // 30
int quotient = a / b;   // 3 (not 3.33! Integer division)
int remainder = a % b;  // 1 (modulo — the leftover)

// To get decimals, make at least one number a double
double accurate = (double) a / b;  // 3.333...

// Shorthand
a++;        // a = a + 1  →  now 11
b--;        // b = b - 1  →  now 2
a += 5;     // a = a + 5  →  now 16
b *= 2;     // b = b * 2  →  now 4
keyPoints: Integer division truncates — 10/3 is 3, not 3.33 (cast to double if you need decimals)
Modulo (%) gives remainder — great for checking even/odd: n % 2 == 0 means even
i++ vs ++i: both add 1, but i++ returns OLD value, ++i returns NEW value
Shorthand operators (+=, -=, *=, /=) are cleaner and slightly faster

concepts.name: Comparison Operators
concepts.explanation: These compare two values and return true or false. Think of them as yes/no questions: 'Is 5 greater than 3?' → true. Use these in if statements to make decisions.
keyPoints: == checks if two things are IDENTICAL (same memory for objects)
equals() checks if two things have the SAME VALUE (use for Strings!)
= is assignment, == is comparison — mixing them up is a classic bug
Don't compare floating-point numbers with == (use Math.abs(a-b) < 0.0001)

concepts.name: Logical Operators
concepts.explanation: These combine multiple conditions. AND (&&) is strict — both must be true. OR (||) is lenient — just one needs to be true. NOT (!) flips true to false and vice versa.
keyPoints: && (AND): True only if BOTH are true — 'You need a ticket AND an ID'
|| (OR): True if AT LEAST ONE is true — 'Cash OR card accepted'
! (NOT): Flips the value — 'Not closed' means open
Short-circuit: && stops at first false, || stops at first true — use for null checks!

concepts.name: Bitwise Operators
concepts.explanation: These work on the individual bits (0s and 1s) inside numbers. Mostly used for low-level programming, flags, and performance tricks. The key insight: shifting left doubles the number, shifting right halves it.
concepts.code:
int a = 5;   // Binary: 0101
int b = 3;   // Binary: 0011

int and = a & b;   // 1  (0101 & 0011 = 0001)
int or = a | b;    // 7  (0101 | 0011 = 0111)
int xor = a ^ b;   // 6  (0101 ^ 0011 = 0110)
int not = ~a;      // -6 (flips all bits)

// Bit shifting — super fast multiplication/division by powers of 2
int doubled = a << 1;   // 10 (shift left = multiply by 2)
int halved = a >> 1;    // 2  (shift right = divide by 2)

// Common use: Checking if a number is odd
boolean isOdd = (n & 1) == 1;  // Faster than n % 2 != 0
keyPoints: << n multiplies by 2ⁿ — much faster than *
>> n divides by 2ⁿ — much faster than /
& with 1 checks if odd — the rightmost bit is 1 only for odd numbers
XOR (^) is reversible: a ^ b ^ b = a — used in simple encryption

concepts.name: If-Else Statements
concepts.explanation: If-else is your basic decision maker. Think of it as a bouncer at a club: 'If you're 21 or older, come in. Else, go home.' Conditions are checked top-to-bottom, and only the first true block runs.
keyPoints: Conditions are checked in order — first true block wins, rest are skipped
else is optional — use when you need a 'default' action
Always use braces {} even for one-liners — prevents bugs when adding code later
Avoid deep nesting — if you have 5+ levels, refactor into methods

concepts.name: Ternary Operator
concepts.explanation: The ternary operator is a one-line if-else. It's like a quick coin flip: 'If heads, do this, else do that.' Great for simple assignments, but don't nest them — it becomes unreadable.
keyPoints: Syntax: condition ? resultIfTrue : resultIfFalse
Perfect for simple either/or assignments
Don't nest ternaries — (a ? (b ? c : d) : e) is unreadable
Both branches must return compatible types

concepts.name: Switch Statement
concepts.explanation: Switch is like a vending machine: you put in a value, and it jumps directly to the matching case. Cleaner than long if-else chains when checking one variable against many values.
keyPoints: break is crucial — without it, execution 'falls through' to the next case
default handles any value not explicitly listed
Works with: int, char, String, and enums (not long or boolean)
Java 14+ arrow syntax (->) is cleaner and doesn't need break

concepts.name: Enhanced Switch Expressions
concepts.explanation: Java 14 introduced switch as an expression — it returns a value directly. No more break statements, no fall-through bugs. Use arrow (->) for clean, one-liner cases.
keyPoints: Arrow syntax (->) replaces colon and break
Multiple cases can share one outcome with commas
Use yield to return a value from a multi-line block
Must be exhaustive — cover all possible values or have default
